items.title: Group A Streptococcus (GAS) and its Clinical Implications
items.content: Group A Streptococcus, or GAS (Streptococcus pyogenes), is a significant pathogen responsible for streptococcal pharyngitis in school-age children. It also leads to serious postinfectious conditions such as acute rheumatic fever and poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: GAS causes streptococcal pharyngitis, a common bacterial infection among school-age children.
Postinfectious syndromes associated with GAS include acute rheumatic fever (ARF) and poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN).
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Infections

items.title: Classification of Streptococci by Hemolytic Patterns and Lancefield Grouping
items.content: Streptococci are classified based on hemolytic patterns (alpha, beta, gamma) and the Lancefield grouping system. Beta-hemolytic streptococci form large colonies (>0.5 mm) on blood agar and can be further categorized by their Lancefield group, which is determined serologically.
keyPoints: Beta-hemolytic streptococci produce a complete hemolysis zone around the colony on blood agar.
Lancefield grouping classifies beta-hemolytic streptococci based on cell-wall carbohydrate antigens.
Groups A, B, C, and G are all beta-hemolytic with specific infection patterns.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Infections

items.title: Impact of Social Distancing on GAS Infections
items.content: The incidence of streptococcal pharyngitis and invasive Group A Streptococcus (GAS) infections decreased during 2020–2022 due to COVID-19-related social distancing. However, a rebound in these infections was observed starting in late 2022, with unusually high rates reported in 2023 and 2024.
keyPoints: COVID-19-related social distancing led to reduced incidence of streptococcal pharyngitis and invasive GAS infections during 2020–2022.
A rebound in GAS infections was observed beginning in late 2022, with high rates reported in 2023 and 2024.
Severe pneumonia, empyema, necrotizing soft tissue infections, and streptococcal toxic shock were prominent among the cases.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · GROUP A STREPTOCOCCI

items.title: Global Burden of GAS Infections and Postinfectious Sequelae
items.content: GAS infections, including postinfectious sequelae like acute rheumatic fever (ARF) and rheumatic heart disease, result in an estimated 500,000 deaths annually worldwide. The incidence is significantly higher in resource-limited countries.
keyPoints: GAS infections and their sequelae account for approximately 500,000 deaths per year globally.
The incidence of GAS infections and rheumatic heart disease is tenfold higher in resource-limited countries compared to developed ones.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · GROUP A STREPTOCOCCI

items.title: M Protein's Role in GAS Immune Evasion and Typing
items.content: The M protein on Group A Streptococcus (GAS) is crucial for its ability to resist phagocytic killing by interfering with complement activation. It also serves as the basis for serotyping, now primarily done via emm gene sequencing.
keyPoints: M protein molecules are fibrillar structures anchored in the cell wall and extend as hairlike projections.
Antibodies to M protein provide protection against subsequent infections of the same M type but not different types.
Emm typing has identified more than 200 emm types, replacing traditional serologic methods.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Hyaluronic Acid Capsule's Role in GAS Pathogenicity
items.content: The hyaluronic acid capsule of GAS protects against phagocytosis but is not essential for virulence, as evidenced by acapsular strains causing invasive infections.
keyPoints: Most clinical isolates produce a hyaluronic acid capsule, except some M types 4, 22, and certain 89.
The capsule's structural similarity to mammalian connective tissue makes it a weak immunogen.
Capsular polysaccharide aids in GAS colonization by binding to CD44 on pharyngeal epithelial cells.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Extracellular Products and Severe Infections Associated with GAS
items.content: GAS produces various extracellular products contributing to local and systemic toxicity, including streptolysins, DNAses, and pyrogenic exotoxins linked to severe infections like necrotizing fasciitis.
keyPoints: Streptolysins S and O cause hemolysis by damaging cell membranes.
Pyrogenic exotoxins are associated with scarlet fever rash and severe invasive infections since the mid-1980s.
Antibodies to streptolysin O and DNase B help diagnose recent streptococcal infection in ARF or PSGN.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Incidence and Presentation of GAS Pharyngitis in Children
items.content: GAS pharyngitis is a common bacterial infection among children, accounting for 20–40% of exudative pharyngitis cases. It's rare under age 3, where symptoms may include fever, malaise, and lymphadenopathy without exudative pharyngitis.
keyPoints: GAS pharyngitis accounts for 20–40% of all exudative pharyngitis in children.
Rare among those under age 3; younger children may present with fever, malaise, and lymphadenopathy without exudative pharyngitis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Streptococcal Pharyngitis
items.content: Diagnosis of streptococcal pharyngitis should not rely solely on clinical grounds due to variable presentations. Throat culture is the gold standard, while rapid diagnostic tests with >95% specificity can confirm diagnosis when positive.
keyPoints: Throat culture remains the diagnostic gold standard for streptococcal pharyngitis.
Rapid diagnostic tests have a specificity of >95%, allowing reliance on positive results for definitive diagnosis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Penicillin Treatment Duration for GAS Pharyngitis to Prevent ARF
items.content: Treatment with penicillin for streptococcal pharyngitis is primarily aimed at preventing suppurative complications and acute rheumatic fever (ARF), rather than merely resolving symptoms. To effectively eradicate the organism from the pharynx and prevent ARF, a 10-day course of penicillin is required.
keyPoints: Penicillin treatment duration for GAS Pharyngitis to prevent ARF: 10 days
Treatment aims primarily at preventing suppurative complications and ARF
Eradication of the organism from the pharynx, not just symptom resolution, is crucial
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · GAS Pharyngitis

items.title: Macrolide Resistance in GAS Treatment
items.content: The use of macrolides such as erythromycin and azithromycin for treating streptococcal pharyngitis is limited by increasing resistance. In areas where resistance rates exceed 5-10%, these antibiotics should be avoided unless susceptibility testing confirms effectiveness.
keyPoints: Macrolide resistance in GAS is common, especially in countries like Spain, Italy, Finland, Japan, and Korea
Resistance rates in the U.S. have risen to >30% since 2021
Avoid macrolides if local resistance rates exceed 5-10%, unless susceptibility testing confirms effectiveness
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · GAS Pharyngitis

items.subtopic: BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS
items.title: Management of Asymptomatic Carriers and Treatment Failure in GAS Infections
items.content: In cases where asymptomatic pharyngeal colonization with Group A Streptococcus (GAS) is identified, or when a patient remains culture-positive after treatment for symptomatic pharyngitis, re-treatment should not be immediately pursued unless symptoms recur. This approach stems from the observation that both the risk of developing acute rheumatic fever and transmitting infection are lower in asymptomatic carriers compared to those with symptomatic pharyngitis.
keyPoints: Up to 20% may have asymptomatic GAS colonization.
Re-treatment is not recommended unless symptoms recur after initial treatment.
Risk of ARF and transmission is lower among asymptomatic carriers.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS

items.subtopic: BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS
items.title: Eradication Strategies for Persistent GAS Carriage
items.content: When asymptomatic carriers are identified as sources of infection, eradication is warranted. If penicillin fails to clear the carriage, alternative regimens include cephalexin or clindamycin, both administered for 10 days. For rectal colonization, a combination of oral vancomycin and rifampin has been effective. Single-dose azithromycin can be used in outbreak scenarios.
keyPoints: Cephalexin: 30 mg/kg (max 500 mg) twice daily for 10 days.
Clindamycin: 7 mg/kg (max 300 mg) three times daily for 10 days.
Vancomycin and rifampin combination for rectal colonization.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS

items.subtopic: BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS
items.title: Scarlet Fever: Clinical Features and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: Scarlet fever is characterized by a streptococcal infection with a distinctive rash, typically arising from toxins produced by the bacteria. The rash begins on the upper trunk and spreads while sparing palms and soles, accompanied by circumoral pallor and 'strawberry tongue.' It subsides in 6–9 days followed by desquamation of palms and soles.
keyPoints: Rash has a 'sandpaper' feel, begins on the upper trunk.
Associated with circumoral pallor and 'strawberry tongue'.
Differential diagnosis includes measles, Kawasaki disease, toxic shock syndrome.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · BACTERIOLOGIC TREATMENT FAILURE AND THE ASYMPTOMATIC CARRIER STATE | Asymptomatic Pharyngeal Colonization with GAS

items.title: Understanding Impetigo Etiology and Presentation
items.content: Impetigo is a superficial skin infection primarily caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) and occasionally by Staphylococcus aureus. It predominantly affects young children, especially in warmer climates or areas with poor hygiene. The infection typically begins as red papules that evolve into vesicular and pustular lesions, forming characteristic honeycomb-like crusts. These lesions are usually not painful, and systemic symptoms like fever suggest deeper tissue involvement or another diagnosis.
keyPoints: Impetigo is primarily caused by GAS and occasionally by S. aureus.
Most common in young children, especially during warmer months.
Lesions evolve from red papules to vesicular and pustular lesions with honeycomb-like crusts.
Fever suggests deeper tissue infection or another diagnosis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · IMPETIGO (PYODERMA)

items.title: Distinguishing Impetigo from Other Skin Lesions
items.content: Impetigo can be confused with herpetic lesions, but they differ in appearance and diagnostic tests. Herpetic lesions appear as discrete, grouped vesicles and are confirmed by a positive Tzanck test or PCR for herpes simplex virus or varicella-zoster virus. In impetigo, cultures of vesicular fluid yield GAS or S. aureus. Bullous impetigo caused by S. aureus is characterized by extensive bullous lesions that form thin paper-like crusts, unlike the thick amber crusts seen in streptococcal impetigo.
keyPoints: Herpetic lesions are discrete, grouped vesicles distinguishable from impetigo.
Positive Tzanck test or PCR confirms herpetic infections.
Cultures of impetigo yield GAS or S. aureus.
Bullous impetigo by S. aureus forms thin paper-like crusts.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · IMPETIGO (PYODERMA)

items.title: Empirical Treatment of Streptococcal Impetigo
items.content: Treatment for streptococcal impetigo should cover both S. aureus and streptococci due to the frequent involvement of S. aureus.
keyPoints: Use dicloxacillin or cephalexin at a dose of 250 mg four times daily for 10 days.
Topical mupirocin ointment is also effective.
Culture may be indicated to rule out methicillin-resistant S. aureus if empirical treatment response is unsatisfactory.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Impetigo | CELLULITIS

items.title: Epidemiology of Acute Rheumatic Fever and Streptococcal Skin Infections
items.content: In certain populations, such as indigenous people in northern Australia and Pacific Island groups, streptococcal impetigo/pyoderma may trigger acute rheumatic fever.
keyPoints: ARF is not typically a sequela to streptococcal skin infections globally.
Strains causing pharyngitis have different M protein types than those causing skin infections.
In specific populations, skin infection may trigger ARF.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Impetigo | CELLULITIS

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Erysipelas
items.content: Erysipelas is a form of streptococcal cellulitis with distinctive clinical features, often requiring broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy.
keyPoints: Erysipelas presents as bright red skin sharply demarcated from normal skin.
Lesions are warm, tender, shiny, swollen, and may have a peau d'orange texture.
Superficial blebs or bullae may form 2–3 days after onset.
Empirical therapy should include coverage for S. aureus if clinical presentation is not typical.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Impetigo | CELLULITIS

items.title: Necrotizing Fasciitis: Identifying and Understanding Pathogenesis
items.content: Necrotizing fasciitis, often caused by hemolytic streptococcus (GAS), involves the superficial or deep fascia of muscles. It can originate from skin trauma or bowel flora during surgery or from an occult source like a diverticular abscess. Polymicrobial cases involve anaerobes and gram-negative bacilli, while GAS alone or with S. aureus is common in non-bowel-related infections.
keyPoints: GAS implicated in ~60% of necrotizing fasciitis cases
Infection may start from skin trauma or bowel flora during surgery
Polymicrobial cases involve anaerobes and gram-negative bacilli
Early symptoms: severe pain, malaise, fever, chills, minimal erythema
Distinguishing feature: severe pain with minimal skin changes
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · DEEP SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS

items.title: Streptococcal Myositis: Recognizing the Fulminant Form
items.content: While S. aureus commonly causes myositis, GAS can lead to streptococcal myositis with minimal fascial or skin involvement. The fulminant form presents subacutely with severe systemic toxicity, bacteremia, and high mortality, resembling necrotizing fasciitis but extending into muscles.
keyPoints: GAS occasionally causes streptococcal myositis
Fulminant form associated with severe systemic toxicity and bacteremia
High mortality rate in fulminant cases
Disease process similar to necrotizing fasciitis but involves muscles
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · DEEP SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS

items.title: Early Surgical Exploration for Necrotizing Fasciitis
items.content: When necrotizing fasciitis is suspected, early surgical exploration is crucial both for diagnosis and treatment. Surgery reveals necrosis and inflammatory fluid along the fascial planes without muscle involvement, often extending beyond clinical signs.
keyPoints: Surgery is diagnostically and therapeutically indicated for necrotizing fasciitis.
Extensive debridement is required due to spread beyond initial clinical area.
Antibiotics are critical but surgery is life-saving.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Deep Soft Tissue Streptococcal Infections

items.title: Management of Streptococcal Myositis
items.content: Streptococcal myositis requires surgical drainage and high-dose penicillin. Clindamycin is recommended due to its efficacy against GAS in dense tissue infections, where beta-lactams are less effective.
keyPoints: Surgical drainage is essential for streptococcal myositis.
High-dose penicillin is part of the treatment regimen.
Clindamycin is preferred due to its effectiveness against GAS in high-density infections.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Deep Soft Tissue Streptococcal Infections

items.title: Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS) Recognition and Management
items.content: Streptococcal TSS, associated with high mortality ($ ≥30	ext{%} $), requires early recognition. It presents with fever, hypotension, renal impairment, and respiratory distress. Most cases are bacteremic and linked to soft tissue infections.
keyPoints: Mortality rate for streptococcal TSS is $≥30	ext{%}$.
Early recognition and aggressive supportive care are crucial.
Associated with soft tissue infections like necrotizing fasciitis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Deep Soft Tissue Streptococcal Infections

items.title: Optimal Antibiotic Regimen for Streptococcal TSS
items.content: High-dose penicillin is the primary treatment for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (TSS). However, clindamycin is recommended as an adjunctive therapy due to its ability to inhibit protein synthesis and potentially reduce toxin production more effectively than penicillin. Clindamycin resistance in over 30% of invasive GAS isolates necessitates susceptibility testing before use.
keyPoints: High-dose penicillin is the agent of choice for streptococcal TSS.
Clindamycin may be more effective than penicillin in terminating toxin production due to its action on protein synthesis.
Clindamycin resistance occurs in 30% or more of invasive GAS isolates, requiring susceptibility testing.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome

items.title: Adjunctive Therapies for Streptococcal TSS
items.content: In cases where clindamycin is not suitable due to resistance, linezolid can be used as an alternative. IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) may also serve as adjunctive therapy by neutralizing streptococcal toxins and modulating inflammatory responses. Although anecdotal evidence suggests benefits, no controlled trials have confirmed its efficacy.
keyPoints: Linezolid is a suitable alternative to clindamycin in cases of resistance or unknown susceptibility.
IVIG may reduce mortality from 33.7% to 15.7%, but lacks prospective trial confirmation.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | STREPTOCOCCI OF GROUPS C AND G
items.title: Vaccine Development for GAS Infections
items.content: Currently, there is no commercially available vaccine against Group A Streptococcus (GAS). However, a recombinant peptide-based vaccine targeting 26 M-protein types has shown promise in early trials by being well tolerated and eliciting type-specific antibody responses. Other vaccines are in development focusing on conserved regions of the M protein or other GAS antigens.
keyPoints: No commercially available vaccine against GAS
Recombinant peptide-based vaccine targeting 26 M-protein types is in phase 1 and 2 trials
Early results show good tolerance and type-specific antibody responses
Other vaccines focus on conserved regions of M protein or other GAS antigens
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | STREPTOCOCCI OF GROUPS C AND G

items.subtopic: ☐ PREVENTION | STREPTOCOCCI OF GROUPS C AND G
items.title: Prophylaxis for Household Contacts of Invasive GAS Infections
items.content: Household contacts of individuals with invasive GAS infections are at increased risk. Asymptomatic pharyngeal colonization can occur in up to 25% of those exposed for more than four hours per day. The CDC does not recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis due to the high number of treatments needed to prevent a single case, but it may be considered for severe cases or high-risk individuals.
keyPoints: Household contacts at increased risk of invasive GAS infection
Asymptomatic pharyngeal colonization in up to 25% with >4 h/d exposure
CDC does not recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis
Prophylaxis may be considered for severe cases or high-risk individuals
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · ☐ PREVENTION | STREPTOCOCCI OF GROUPS C AND G

items.subtopic: Group C or G Streptococcal Infection | GROUP B STREPTOCOCCI
items.title: Treatment Approach for Group C or G Streptococcal Infections
items.content: Penicillin is the primary treatment for group C or G streptococcal infections, similar to GAS. IV penicillin (2–4 mU every 4 h) is recommended for bacteremia or septic arthritis due to its efficacy against these organisms, which are inhibited in vitro by concentrations of ≤0.03 µg/mL. Some isolates exhibit tolerance, necessitating higher penicillin concentrations for eradication. In cases like endocarditis or septic arthritis, the addition of gentamicin (1 mg/kg every 8 h) is recommended for some patients, although it has not been proven superior to penicillin alone.
keyPoints: Penicillin is the drug of choice for group C or G streptococcal infections.
IV penicillin dosage: 2–4 mU every 4 hours for bacteremia or septic arthritis.
Group C and G streptococci are inhibited in vitro by ≤0.03 µg/mL of penicillin.
Some isolates show tolerance, requiring higher penicillin concentrations.
Gentamicin (1 mg/kg every 8 hours) is recommended for endocarditis or septic arthritis but not proven superior to penicillin alone.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Group C or G Streptococcal Infection | GROUP B STREPTOCOCCI

items.subtopic: Group C or G Streptococcal Infection | GROUP B STREPTOCOCCI
items.title: Diagnosis and Epidemiology of Group B Streptococcus (GBS) Infections
items.content: Group B streptococcus, identified as S. agalactiae, is a major cause of neonatal sepsis and meningitis, peripartum fever in women, and serious infections in nonpregnant adults with chronic illnesses. Prenatal screening has reduced the incidence of neonatal infection from ~2–3 cases to ~0.6 cases per 1000 live births. Diagnosis involves biochemical tests: positive sodium hippurate hydrolysis (99%), negative bile esculin hydrolysis (99-100%), bacitracin resistance (92%), and CAMP factor production (98-100%). GBS infections are encapsulated by one of ten antigenically distinct polysaccharides, which is a key virulence factor.
keyPoints: GBS identified as S. agalactiae causes neonatal sepsis, meningitis, peripartum fever, and serious adult infections.
Prenatal screening reduced neonatal GBS infection incidence from ~2–3 to ~0.6 cases per 1000 live births.
Diagnostic tests: positive sodium hippurate hydrolysis (99%), negative bile esculin hydrolysis (99-100%), bacitracin resistance (92%), CAMP factor production (98-100%).
GBS infections are encapsulated by one of ten antigenically distinct polysaccharides, a key virulence factor.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Group C or G Streptococcal Infection | GROUP B STREPTOCOCCI

items.title: Early-Onset Group B Streptococcus Infection in Neonates: Risk Factors and Presentation
items.content: Early-onset GBS infection occurs within the first week of life, typically presenting at a median age of 20 hours. It is acquired from colonized maternal genital tracts during or shortly before birth. Key risk factors include prematurity, prolonged labor, obstetric complications, and maternal fever.
keyPoints: Early-onset infection occurs within the first week of life with a median onset at 20 hours.
5–40% of women are carriers of GBS in their vaginal or rectal tracts.
50% of infants born vaginally to carrier mothers become colonized, but only 1–2% develop clinically evident infection.
Risk factors: prematurity, prolonged labor, obstetric complications, maternal fever.
Presentation includes respiratory distress, lethargy, hypotension; essentially all are bacteremic.
One-third to one-half have pneumonia/respiratory distress syndrome; one-third have meningitis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · INFECTION IN NEONATES

items.title: Late-Onset Group B Streptococcus Infection in Neonates: Presentation and Common Manifestations
items.content: Late-onset GBS infection occurs from 1 week to 3 months of age, with a mean onset at 3–4 weeks. It can be acquired during delivery or later contact with colonized individuals. Meningitis is the most common manifestation, often associated with capsular type III strains.
keyPoints: Late-onset infection occurs from 1 week to 3 months of age (mean onset at 3–4 weeks).
Acquired during delivery or later contact with colonized individuals.
Meningitis is the most common manifestation, often associated with capsular type III strains.
Presentation includes fever, lethargy/irritability, poor feeding, seizures.
Other manifestations: bacteremia without an identified source, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, facial cellulitis with submandibular or preauricular adenitis.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · INFECTION IN NEONATES

items.subtopic: Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates
items.title: Penicillin as First-Line Treatment for GBS Infections
items.content: Penicillin is the preferred treatment for all Group B Streptococcus (GBS) infections, including bacteremia and meningitis. For neonatal meningitis, dosing varies based on age: infants ≤7 days receive 250,000–450,000 units/kg per day in three divided doses; those >7 days receive 450,000–500,000 units/kg per day in four divided doses.
keyPoints: Penicillin is the agent of choice for GBS infections.
Infants with bacteremia or soft tissue infection should receive penicillin at a dosage of 200,000 units/kg per day in divided doses.
For meningitis: ≤7 days old infants require 250,000–450,000 units/kg/day; >7 days old infants need 450,000–500,000 units/kg/day.
Meningitis should be treated for at least 14 days to prevent relapse.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates

items.subtopic: Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates
items.title: Prevention Strategies and Risk Factors for GBS
items.content: The incidence of neonatal GBS infection is high among infants born to mothers with specific risk factors such as preterm delivery, prolonged labor, or chorioamnionitis. Screening at 35–37 weeks via swab culture can help identify carriers, guiding prophylactic antibiotic administration during delivery.
keyPoints: High-risk factors for neonatal GBS include preterm delivery, early rupture of membranes, prolonged labor, fever, and chorioamnionitis.
Screening for anogenital colonization is recommended at 35–37 weeks with swab cultures.
Intrapartum chemoprophylaxis is advised for culture-positive women or those with a history of GBS-related complications.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates

items.subtopic: Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates
items.title: Chemoprophylaxis Regimen and Alternatives for Penicillin Allergy
items.content: The standard chemoprophylaxis regimen involves a loading dose of 5 million units of penicillin G, followed by 2.5 million units every 4 hours until delivery. For those with penicillin allergy not at high risk for anaphylaxis, cefazolin is recommended; clindamycin can be used if the isolate is susceptible, otherwise vancomycin should be administered.
keyPoints: Standard chemoprophylaxis: 5 million units loading dose of penicillin G followed by 2.5 million units every 4 hours until delivery.
Cefazolin is an alternative for women with non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy.
Clindamycin may be used if the isolate is susceptible; otherwise, vancomycin should be administered.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Group B Streptococcal Infection in Neonates

items.subtopic: INFECTION IN ADULTS | Group B Streptococcal Infection in Adults
items.title: Penicillin Dosing for Serious GBS Infections in Adults
items.content: For adults with serious localized GBS infections, such as pneumonia and pyelonephritis, higher doses of penicillin are required due to reduced sensitivity compared to GAS. Divided doses of approximately 12 million units daily are recommended. For more severe cases like endocarditis or meningitis, the dosage increases to 18–24 million units per day.
keyPoints: GBS is less sensitive to penicillin than GAS.
Serious localized infections require ~12 million units of penicillin G daily in divided doses.
Endocarditis or meningitis requires 18–24 million units per day in divided doses.
Vancomycin is an alternative for penicillin-allergic patients.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · INFECTION IN ADULTS | Group B Streptococcal Infection in Adults | NONENTERO COCCAL GROUP D STREPTOCOCCI

items.subtopic: INFECTION IN ADULTS | NONENTERO COCCAL GROUP D STREPTOCOCCI
items.title: Association of Nonenterococcal Group D Streptococci with Gastrointestinal Neoplasms
items.content: Nonenterococcal group D streptococci, including species like S. gallolyticus and S. infantarius, are linked to endocarditis often associated with gastrointestinal neoplasms such as colon carcinoma or polyps. More than 60% of patients with endocarditis due to these organisms have occult gastrointestinal lesions when investigated.
keyPoints: Endocarditis by nonenterococcal group D streptococci is linked to GI tract neoplasms, especially colon carcinoma or polyps.
>60% of patients with endocarditis from S. gallolyticus or S. infantarius have occult GI lesions upon investigation.
Penicillin alone effectively treats infections caused by these organisms.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · INFECTION IN ADULTS | Group B Streptococcal Infection in Adults | NONENTERO COCCAL GROUP D STREPTOCOCCI

items.title: Viridans Streptococci as Agents of Bacterial Endocarditis
items.content: Viridans streptococci, part of the normal oral flora, are significant agents in bacterial endocarditis due to their ability to adhere to biological surfaces following transient bacteremia from minor oral trauma.
keyPoints: Viridans streptococci include species such as Streptococcus salivarius, S. mitis, S. sanguis, and S. mutans.
Transient viridans streptococcal bacteremia can occur from activities like eating or toothbrushing.
These organisms have a predilection for causing endocarditis due to adherence to biologic surfaces after transient bacteremia.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · VIRIDANS STREPTOCOCCI

items.title: Viridans Streptococcal Bacteremia in Neutropenic Patients
items.content: Neutropenic patients, especially those undergoing bone marrow transplantation or high-dose chemotherapy, are at increased risk for viridans streptococcal bacteremia, which can lead to severe sepsis syndrome.
keyPoints: Viridans streptococcal bacteremia is common in neutropenic patients post-bone marrow transplant or high-dose chemotherapy.
Risk factors include prior use of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, fluoroquinolones, antacids, histamine antagonists, mucositis, and profound neutropenia.
Patients may develop a sepsis syndrome characterized by high fever and shock.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · VIRIDANS STREPTOCOCCI

items.subtopic: Infection with Viridans Streptococci | ABIOTROPHIA AND GRANULICATELLA SPECIES (NUTRITIONALLY VARIANT STREPTOCOCCI) | Infection with Nutritionally Variant Streptococci
items.title: Presumptive Treatment for Neutropenic Patients with Viridans Streptococcal Bacteremia
items.content: In neutropenic patients presenting with bacteremia caused by viridans streptococci, presumptive treatment should begin with vancomycin due to the high likelihood of penicillin resistance. Susceptibility testing is crucial for guiding further treatment.
keyPoints: Viridans streptococci in neutropenic patients are often resistant to penicillin.
Presumptive treatment with vancomycin is recommended until susceptibility results are available.
Susceptibility testing should guide the treatment of serious infections.
items.reference: Streptococcal Infections · Infection with Viridans Streptococci | ABIOTROPHIA AND GRANULICATELLA SPECIES (NUTRITIONALLY VARIANT STREPTOCOCCI) | Infection with Nutritionally Variant Streptococci | OTHER STREPTOCOCCI

items.subtopic: ABIOTROPHIA AND GRANULICATELLA SPECIES (NUTRITIONALLY VARIANT STREPTOCOCCI) | Infection with Nutritionally Variant Streptococci
items.title: Management of Endocarditis Due to Nutritionally Variant Streptococci
items.content: Endocarditis caused by nutritionally variant streptococci, such as Abiotrophia and Granulicatella species, is associated with higher rates of treatment failure and relapse. The addition of gentamicin (1 mg/kg every 8 hours for patients with normal renal function) to a penicillin regimen is recommended.
keyPoints: Nutritionally variant streptococci include Abiotrophia defectiva and Granulicatella species.
Endocarditis due to these organisms has higher rates of treatment failure and relapse compared to usual viridans streptococci.
Addition of gentamicin (1 mg/kg every 8 hours) to penicillin is recommended for endocarditis caused by nutritionally variant streptococci.

items.reason: Rapid diagnostic tests are less sensitive than throat cultures, with a relative sensitivity of 70–90%.
